common_name: White Calanthe (White Nun Orchid, Triple-Winged Calanthe, Evergreen Calanthe)

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs or container-grown divisions in spring in partial to full shade in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Calanthe triplicata is one of the most cold-tolerant Calanthe species, surviving brief temperatures to 28–30°F with mulch protection in zone 9a. It is evergreen (unlike the deciduous Japanese ebine calanthes), requiring moisture year-round. The tall spikes of white flowers with orange-red centers are produced in spring. Excellent for naturalistic shade gardens under large trees. Divide every 3–4 years. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — White Calanthe thrives in the deep, moist, organic-rich conditions of a shaded garden bed under large trees and will decline in dry or sunny positions. Calanthe triplicata is a clump-forming terrestrial orchid with an exceptionally wide natural range — one of the most geographically widespread of all orchid species, native from Japan, China, and India through all of Southeast Asia to Australia, New Zealand, and many Pacific Islands in the Orchidaceae family. The plant produces robust, oval pseudobulbs and 3–4 broad, pleated, rich-green leaves (12–20 inches) per pseudobulb. In spring, one or more erect flower spikes 18–36 inches tall emerge from the base, each bearing 10–25 flowers in a showy raceme. The flowers are white to pure white, approximately 1.5 inches across, with the central lip distinctly yellow-orange to orange at the center and three-lobed — the 'triplicata' (triple) refers to this three-lobed lip structure. The two-toned white and orange center creates a clean, elegant flower display. As one of the most cold-tolerant of the Calanthe species (rated to zone 9), and as an evergreen species requiring no winter rest period, C. triplicata is among the most adaptable and reliable of the terrestrial orchids for zone 9-12 shade garden planting. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Calanthe triplicata cultivation guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Terrestrial Orchids for Florida shade gardens
LSU AgCenter — Cold-Hardy Terrestrial Orchids for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 9
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Calanthe triplicata species profile

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light in well-draining bark-based medium (not mounted); Zygopetalum mackayi grows naturally at moderate elevations (2,000–5,000 feet) in the Atlantic Forest of Brazil and appreciates cooler nights (50–60°F) that are essential for triggering bloom — without cool nights in fall and winter, the plant may not flower. Water regularly during active growth; reduce in winter. Blooms fall–winter, emitting a powerful, sweet, hyacinth-like fragrance. One of the more cold-tolerant sympodial epiphytic orchids. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light in well-draining bark medium — Zygopetalum mackayi needs good light to bloom and will not flower in dim conditions, but is damaged by direct summer sun on the foliage. Zygopetalum mackayi is a sympodial, epiphytic to semi-terrestrial orchid native to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil (Minas Gerais, Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo) at elevations of 2,000–5,000 feet in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most distinctive and ornamentally striking orchids in terms of flower coloration. The flowers of Zygopetalum are immediately recognizable: large (3–4 inches), with three sepals and two petals in the same rich coloration — a combination of green with broad bands and blotches of chocolate-brown to deep maroon — and a dramatically contrasting broad, spreading lip in pure white to pale lilac heavily marked with bold veins of deep purple to dark violet, creating a complex pattern of purple lines and dots on a white or pale background. The color combination of maroon-spotted green petals against a purple-veined white lip is unique among commonly grown orchids and immediately eye-catching. The flowers are strongly, sweetly fragrant — a powerful, hyacinth-like scent that fills a room. Multiple flowers per spike (5–12), with tall erect spikes in fall and winter. A prized and underused orchid for zone 9-11 collectors who can provide cool nights in fall to trigger bloom. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Zygopetalum mackayi cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Zygopetalum mackayi species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Zygopetalum cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Zygopetalum (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Cinnamon Orchid (Aromatic Lycaste, Yellow Lycaste, Spice Orchid)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light in bark-based medium in a clay or wooden container; Lycaste aromatica is deciduous — it sheds its large leaves in winter after a dry rest period, and this dry period (reducing water in fall as growth slows) is essential for triggering the spring bloom. Flowers emerge on short stems directly from the leafless pseudobulbs in spring before new growth starts. The cinnamon-spice fragrance is powerful and carries. Tolerates temperatures to 45–50°F briefly — one of the more cold-tolerant Lycaste species. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light in well-draining bark medium — Cinnamon Orchid needs good light for pseudobulb development and to fuel the next year's bloom, but should be protected from intense direct midday sun that scorches the large, broad leaves. Lycaste aromatica is a deciduous, sympodial epiphytic orchid native to Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, and El Salvador in the Orchidaceae family, grown for flowers with one of the most immediately recognizable and pleasant fragrances of any commonly cultivated orchid — a clean, warm, distinctly cinnamon-spice fragrance that smells precisely of ground cinnamon sticks with a hint of honey, and that carries powerfully several feet from the plant on warm days. The plant produces conical, angular pseudobulbs with 2–3 large, broad, pleated leaves (18–24 inches) that drop in fall. From the leafless pseudobulbs in spring, numerous short flower spikes emerge simultaneously — a well-established plant may produce 15–25 or more individual spikes at once, each carrying a single, 2-inch, upward-facing flower in clear, bright canary-yellow to golden-yellow with a small, darker orange lip. The display of dozens of fragrant yellow flowers simultaneously emerging from leafless pseudobulbs in a mass burst of spring bloom is one of the most memorable of any sympodial orchid. A forgiving and adaptable orchid that tolerates cooler temperatures than many tropical species. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Lycaste aromatica cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Lycaste aromatica species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Lycaste cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Lycaste (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: African Spiderflower (Spiderwisp, Cat's Whiskers Vegetable, Mchicha wa Pori)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun in any well-drained garden soil; Gynandropsis gynandra grows quickly and can be harvested for greens beginning 4–6 weeks after seeding. The young leaves, tender shoot tips, and flower buds are all edible — boil or steam briefly (the raw leaves can be bitter) and serve with oil or sauce as a nutritious cooked leafy green. It is one of the most nutritious leafy greens available in hot-season gardens, rich in protein, iron, calcium, and vitamins. Self-seeds freely. Closely related to Cleome but now classified in a separate genus. Widely grown as a vegetable throughout sub-Saharan Africa.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — African Spiderflower germinates rapidly in warm soil and begins producing harvestable leaves within 4–6 weeks. Gynandropsis gynandra (formerly Cleome gynandra) is a fast-growing, warm-season annual native throughout tropical Africa, South and Southeast Asia in the Cleomaceae family, one of the most nutritionally important leafy vegetables in the diets of millions of people throughout sub-Saharan Africa, where it is known by many names including 'mchicha wa pori' (Swahili), 'mbikira' (Uganda), and 'nyanya' (parts of West Africa). The plant produces compound leaves of 3–5 leaflets on upright, slightly sticky, aromatic stems, terminating in elongated clusters of small white to pale purple flowers with prominent, long, spider-like stamens — the spider-flower appearance is characteristic of the Cleomaceae family. The young leaves and shoot tips, harvested before the plant flowers, are boiled or stir-fried as a nutritious cooked vegetable — the flavor is slightly bitter and cabbage-like when raw, becoming mildly pleasant when cooked. Nutritional analyses document exceptionally high protein content (the highest of commonly grown leafy vegetables), high iron, calcium, and riboflavin levels — properties that have made G. gynandra the subject of food security research as a nutrient-dense leafy green for hot climates where cool-season greens cannot be grown. Self-seeds prolifically in zone 9-12 gardens once established.
sources: Food and Agriculture Organization — Gynandropsis gynandra: nutrient-dense African vegetable
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical leafy greens for Gulf Coast zone 9-10
Purdue University NewCROP — Gynandropsis gynandra (Spider Flower Vegetable)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical leafy greens for zone 9 summer gardens

common_name: Carnivorous Bromeliad (Lantern Bromeliad, Berterone's Catopsis, Tank Trap Bromeliad)

planting.note: Grow mounted on bark or cork, or in a small pot with bromeliad bark mix, in partial shade with good air circulation; Catopsis berteroniana is frost-sensitive and suited to zone 10-12 outdoor culture or protected porch/indoor culture in zone 9. Keep the central tank (cup) filled with rainwater or distilled water — the waxy, pale yellow-white powdery coating on the inner tank walls may trap insects that slide in and drown, contributing to the plant's nutrition. Clean the tank occasionally to remove debris. Propagate from offsets.

directions: Grow in partial shade to bright indirect light in bromeliad bark medium or mounted on bark — Carnivorous Bromeliad performs best in the dappled light of a shaded outdoor position or bright indoor location with good air circulation. Catopsis berteroniana is a small to medium-sized epiphytic bromeliad native to southern Florida (where it is endangered), the Caribbean, Central America, and South America in the Bromeliaceae family, noteworthy as the only bromeliad species with a convincing scientific case for genuine carnivory. The evidence, documented in ecological studies, is based on a distinctive combination of features not found in other bromeliads: the smooth, very steeply angled inner walls of the central tank are coated with an unusual white, powdery, waxy substance (trichomes) that reflects UV light — attracting insects — while simultaneously making the walls so slippery that trapped insects cannot escape. The drowned insects accumulate in the tank and are thought to be digested by the plant's absorptive trichomes. While not as definitively carnivorous as Sarracenia or Nepenthes, the adaptation appears functional. The plant itself is attractive: pale yellow-green to whitish foliage forms an open, lantern-like rosette. It produces a tall, erect inflorescence with small, white to yellow flowers. An unusual and scientifically interesting collector's bromeliad.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Catopsis berteroniana: Florida native bromeliad
Florida Natural Areas Inventory — Catopsis berteroniana conservation status
Joel, D.M. et al. — Evidence for carnivory in Catopsis berteroniana
Bromeliad Society International — Catopsis berteroniana cultivation notes
